serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of steamed white rice in Singapore is approximately one rice bowl, weighing about 180 g cooked.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, white, cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cooked white rice (G00010504). The input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95. Sodium (231 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g) are atypically high for plain steamed white rice; these values are preserved as reported by HPB and may reflect a seasoned or mixed-grain rice preparation. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20444, cooked enriched white rice) reports saturated fat at 0.19 g/100 g and is used as a secondary source for that field only. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 for white rice (cooked, boiled) is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 45.34) / 100 ≈ 32.7, based on a 180 g serving containing 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g (i.e., 45.34 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium (231 mg/100 g) atypical for plain steamed rice; potassium (327 mg/100 g) partially offsets sodium load
hypertension.guidance: The sodium content of 231 mg/100 g in this HPB-recorded rice entry is unusually high for plain steamed white rice and may reflect a seasoned or mixed preparation; individuals with hypertension should verify the preparation method and limit portion size to manage sodium intake, while the potassium content of 327 mg/100 g provides a favourable Na:K ratio that partially supports blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and high glycemic load per serving (~32.7) drive rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: White rice has a high GI of 72 and a glycemic load of approximately 32.7 per standard 180 g serving, making portion control essential for individuals with type 2 diabetes; pairing rice with high-fibre vegetables, legumes, or lean protein can blunt the glycaemic response, and substituting brown rice or mixed-grain rice is recommended to reduce overall glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with high glycemic load; low saturated fat but elevated sodium in this HPB entry
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain white rice is low in saturated fat (0.19 g/100 g) and cholesterol-free, which is favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the high glycemic load and the atypically elevated sodium (231 mg/100 g) in this HPB entry warrant portion control and preparation verification for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed white rice (米饭, Nasi Putih) is a foundational staple food consumed daily across Singapore, China, and much of Southeast Asia. It is prepared by washing polished white rice grains and cooking them with water through steaming or absorption boiling until soft and slightly sticky. The texture is tender and cohesive, with a mild, neutral flavour that complements a wide range of savoury dishes. In Singapore, steamed white rice is served at virtually every Chinese, Malay, and Indian meal, forming the carbohydrate base of dishes such as Hainanese chicken rice, char siew rice, and economy rice (cai fan). Nutritionally, this HPB-recorded entry provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g cooked. The sodium (231 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g) values are atypically high for plain steamed white rice and may reflect a seasoned or mixed-grain preparation. White rice has a high glycemic index of approximately 72, making glycemic load management through portion control and food pairing critical for individuals with type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, making it suitable across most dietary requirements. A standard serving in Singapore is approximately one rice bowl (180 g cooked).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010504) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 72 is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for cooked white rice. Saturated fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a secondary source for that field only. The atypically high sodium and fibre values are preserved as reported by HPB with appropriate caveats documented in notes.
notes: 1. ATYPICAL NUTRIENT VALUES: The HPB entry for G00010504 reports sodium at 231 mg/100 g and dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g, which are significantly higher than typical values for plain steamed white rice (sodium ~1–5 mg/100 g; dietary fibre ~0.4 g/100 g cooked). These values are preserved as reported by the approved source (HPB) and may reflect a seasoned, fortified, or mixed-grain rice preparation. Downstream users should verify the preparation method before applying these values to plain white rice. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this record and detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture data are not available in the HPB database for this specific entry as of 2026. 3. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g (0.19 g/100 g) is supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20444, white rice cooked enriched) as a secondary source, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 4. CHOLESTEROL: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; rice is a plant food and contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). 5. GLYCEMIC INDEX: GI of 72 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for cooked white rice. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 45.34 g carbohydrate per 180 g serving) / 100 ≈ 32.7. 6. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches for cooked white rice; FoodEx2 code A000E is used as the closest defensible match for cooked rice in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
